examples.title: Five-Stage Pipeline Execution Simulation
examples.description: Simulates the execution of instructions through a 5-stage pipeline showing pipeline stages and cycles
examples.code:
MODULE PipelineSimulation
BEGIN
  stages = ["IF", "ID", "EX", "MEM", "WB"]
  pipeline = ARRAY[5] initially EMPTY
  instruction_count = INPUT("Enter number of instructions: ")
  clock_cycles = 0
  
  FOR i = 1 TO instruction_count DO
    // Shift instructions in pipeline
    FOR j = 4 DOWNTO 1 DO
      pipeline[j+1] = pipeline[j]
    END FOR
    // Insert new instruction at IF stage
    pipeline[1] = "Instruction_" + i
    clock_cycles = clock_cycles + 1
    
    PRINT "Cycle", clock_cycles, ":", pipeline
  END FOR
  
  // Drain remaining instructions
  FOR drain = 1 TO 4 DO
    FOR j = 4 DOWNTO 1 DO
      pipeline[j+1] = pipeline[j]
    END FOR
    pipeline[1] = "EMPTY"
    clock_cycles = clock_cycles + 1
    PRINT "Cycle", clock_cycles, ":", pipeline
  END FOR
  
  PRINT "Total clock cycles for", instruction_count, "instructions:", clock_cycles
  PRINT "Ideal CPI:", clock_cycles / instruction_count
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates how instructions flow through a 5-stage pipeline. Each clock cycle shifts instructions to the next stage while inserting new instructions at IF. After all instructions are inserted, the pipeline is drained. The total cycles = n + k - 1 for n instructions in k-stage pipeline.

examples.title: Pipeline Data Hazard Detection (RAW)
examples.description: Algorithm to detect and handle Read-After-Write data hazards in pipeline
examples.code:
MODULE DetectRAWHazard
BEGIN
  // Each instruction has: dest_reg, src1_reg, src2_reg, stage
  instruction_queue = []
  register_status = ARRAY[32] initially -1 // -1 means free
  
  FUNCTION check_hazard(current_inst)
    FOR each inst IN instruction_queue DO
      IF inst.stage >= ID AND inst.stage <= WB THEN
        // Check if source register is destination of previous instruction
        IF current_inst.src1_reg == inst.dest_reg THEN
          RETURN TRUE // RAW hazard detected
        END IF
        IF current_inst.src2_reg == inst.dest_reg THEN
          RETURN TRUE // RAW hazard detected
        END IF
      END IF
    END FOR
    RETURN FALSE
  END FUNCTION
  
  FUNCTION resolve_hazard_with_forwarding(current_inst)
    FOR each inst IN instruction_queue DO
      IF inst.stage == EX OR inst.stage == MEM THEN
        IF current_inst.src1_reg == inst.dest_reg THEN
          // Forward from EX/MEM pipeline register
          current_inst.src1_value = inst.alu_result
          RETURN // Hazard resolved
        END IF
      END IF
      IF inst.stage == MEM AND inst.stage == WB THEN
        IF current_inst.src2_reg == inst.dest_reg THEN
          current_inst.src2_value = inst.alu_result
          RETURN
        END IF
      END IF
    END FOR
  END FUNCTION
END
examples.explanation: This code shows how to detect RAW hazards by checking if any instruction currently in the pipeline will write to a register that the current instruction needs to read. It also demonstrates forwarding logic to resolve hazards without stalling.

examples.title: Pipeline Performance Calculator
examples.description: Python program to calculate ideal and actual CPI, speedup, and execution time for pipelined processor
examples.code:
def pipeline_performance(num_stages, num_instructions, clock_time_ns, branch_freq=0.2, branch_penalty=2, other_stalls=0):
    """
    Calculate pipeline performance metrics
    
    Args:
        num_stages: Number of pipeline stages (k)
        num_instructions: Number of instructions (n)
        clock_time_ns: Clock cycle time in nanoseconds
        branch_freq: Frequency of branch instructions
        branch_penalty: Cycles lost per mispredicted branch
        other_stalls: Average other stalls per instruction
    """
    # Ideal case (no hazards)
    ideal_cycles = num_stages + num_instructions - 1
    ideal_cpi = ideal_cycles / num_instructions
    ideal_time = ideal_cycles * clock_time_ns
    
    # Actual case with hazards
    stall_cycles_per_branch = branch_freq * branch_penalty
    total_stalls = stall_cycles_per_branch + other_stalls
    actual_cpi = 1 + total_stalls  # Base CPI = 1 for pipeline
    actual_cycles = int(actual_cpi * num_instructions)
    actual_time = actual_cycles * clock_time_ns
    
    # Speedup calculation
    # Non-pipelined: each instruction takes k cycles
    non_pipelined_cycles = num_stages * num_instructions
    speedup = non_pipelined_cycles / actual_cycles
    
    # Throughput
    throughput = 1000 / actual_time  # Instructions per second (in millions)
    
    print(f"PIPELINE PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS")
    print(f"{'='*40}")
    print(f"Pipeline Stages: {num_stages}")
    print(f"Instructions: {num_instructions}")
    print(f"Clock Time: {clock_time_ns} ns")
    print(f"\nIDEAL PERFORMANCE:")
    print(f"  Total Cycles: {ideal_cycles}")
    print(f"  CPI: {ideal_cpi:.4f}")
    print(f"  Execution Time: {ideal_time} ns")
    print(f"\nACTUAL PERFORMANCE:")
    print(f"  Branch Frequency: {branch_freq*100}%")
    print(f"  Branch Penalty: {branch_penalty} cycles")
    print(f"  Total Stalls: {total_stalls:.4f} per instruction")
    print(f"  Actual CPI: {actual_cpi:.4f}")
    print(f"  Total Cycles: {actual_cycles}")
    print(f"  Execution Time: {actual_time} ns")
    print(f"\nSPEEDUP: {speedup:.2f}x")
    print(f"THROUGHPUT: {throughput:.2f} MIPS")
    
    return {
        'ideal_cpi': ideal_cpi,
        'actual_cpi': actual_cpi,
        'speedup': speedup,
        'throughput': throughput
    }

# Example usage
result = pipeline_performance(
    num_stages=5,
    num_instructions=1000,
    clock_time_ns=2,
    branch_freq=0.2,
    branch_penalty=3,
    other_stalls=0.1
)
examples.explanation: This Python program calculates various pipeline performance metrics including ideal CPI, actual CPI with hazards, speedup over non-pipelined execution, and throughput. It demonstrates how branch frequency and penalty affect overall pipeline performance.

examples.title: Branch Prediction State Machine
examples.description: Simple 2-bit branch predictor to reduce control hazard penalty
examples.code:
MODULE BranchPredictor
BEGIN
  // 2-bit saturating counter for branch prediction
  // States: 00 = Strong Not Taken, 01 = Weak Not Taken
  //         10 = Weak Taken, 11 = Strong Taken
  
  CONSTANTS
    STRONG_NOT_TAKEN = 0
    WEAK_NOT_TAKEN = 1
    WEAK_TAKEN = 2
    STRONG_TAKEN = 3
  
  branch_table = HASH_TABLE // Indexed by branch PC
  
  FUNCTION predict(branch_address)
    IF branch_table.contains(branch_address) THEN
      state = branch_table.get(branch_address)
    ELSE
      state = WEAK_NOT_TAKEN  // Initial prediction
      branch_table.put(branch_address, state)
    END IF
    
    // Return prediction: TRUE = taken, FALSE = not taken
    IF state >= WEAK_TAKEN THEN
      RETURN TRUE
    ELSE
      RETURN FALSE
    END IF
  END FUNCTION
  
  FUNCTION update(branch_address, actually_taken)
    state = branch_table.get(branch_address)
    
    IF actually_taken THEN
      // Move towards taken state
      IF state < STRONG_TAKEN THEN
        state = state + 1
      END IF
    ELSE
      // Move towards not taken state
      IF state > STRONG_NOT_TAKEN THEN
        state = state - 1
      END IF
    END IF
    
    branch_table.put(branch_address, state)
  END FUNCTION
  
  // Example: Simulate branch prediction
  test_branches = [FALSE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE]
  prediction_count = 0
  correct_count = 0
  
  FOR each outcome IN test_branches DO
    pred = predict(0x1000) // Example branch address
    prediction_count = prediction_count + 1
    IF pred == outcome THEN
      correct_count = correct_count + 1
    END IF
    update(0x1000, outcome)
  END FOR
  
  PRINT "Branch Prediction Accuracy:", correct_count, "/", prediction_count
END
examples.explanation: This code implements a 2-bit saturating counter branch predictor. It uses a branch history table indexed by branch instruction address. The predictor uses hysteresis (2 bits) to avoid thrashing on occasional mispredictions, reducing control hazard penalties in pipeline processors.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m) where m is number of branch instructions in table
